# Concept Card: Architecture

## Definition
Transcript evidence থেকে derived canonical definition; conflicting views থাকলে সব variant রাখা হয়েছে।

## First Principles
System boundary, control loop, এবং failure behavior দৃষ্টিতে concept ব্যাখ্যা করতে হবে।

## Mental Model
এই concept-কে runtime contract হিসেবে ধরলে debugging ও design decisions সহজ হয়।

## Why It Exists
Operational complexity কমানো এবং reliable automation enable করা।

## Internal Working
Control plane state reconciliation + node-level execution pathway।

## YAML and Command Usage
Related raw commands এবং manifests reference-based রাখা হয়েছে; no rewrite of original evidence.

## Use Cases
Lab, production-like demos, and certification patterns থেকে mapped।

## Edge Cases
Version mismatch, policy conflict, scheduling/storage/network constraints উল্লেখ করা হয়েছে।

## Pitfalls
Warning/Pitfall entries evidence সহ retain করা হয়েছে।

## Troubleshooting Angle
Symptom -> Cause -> Fix logic chain evidence-based maintain করা হয়েছে।

## Exam Relevance
CKA/CKAD speed + correctness + troubleshooting expectations mapped।

## Source Contributions

### Evidence 38: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/007_Cluster Architecture.extraction.md::E30
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 007_Cluster Architecture.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It identifies the right ship based on its size, its capacity, the number of containers already on the ship, and any other conditions such as the destination of the ship, the type of containers it is allowed to carry, etc. so those are schedulers.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 39: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/007_Cluster Architecture.extraction.md::E31
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 007_Cluster Architecture.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In a Kubernetes cluster, a scheduler identifies the right node to place a container on based on the containers resource requirements, the worker nodes capacity, or any other policies or constraints such as taints and tolerations or node affinity rules that are on them.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture

- Type: Architecture

- Content: The older API, which used etcd cuddle set and etcd cuddle get commands instead of etcd cuddle put and get commands.

### Evidence 185: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E81
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 010_ETCD For Beginners.txt

### Evidence 186: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E82
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 010_ETCD For Beginners.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 187: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E83
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 010_ETCD For Beginners.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In November 2018, etcd became a CNCF incubator project, meaning the Cloud Native Computing Foundation took ownership of governing and supporting the project, and more versions were released through to November 2020, when etcd became a CNCF graduated project.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 188: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E84
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 010_ETCD For Beginners.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In June 2021, version 3.5 was released.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 189: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E85
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 010_ETCD For Beginners.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the point I'm trying to make is that you might see blogs documentation online regarding the older API, which used etcd cuddle set and get commands instead of the etcd cuddle put and get commands, so don't be alarmed.

### Evidence 190: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E86

### Evidence 192: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/010_ETCD For Beginners.extraction.md::E88

### Evidence 193: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/011_ETCD in Kubernetes.extraction.md::E1
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 011_ETCD in Kubernetes.txt

- Content: In the previous lecture, we discussed the basics of etcd.

### Evidence 273: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As we discussed earlier, the Kube controller manager manages various controllers in Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 274: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A controller is like an office or department within the master ship that have their own set of responsibilities, such as an office for the ships would be responsible for monitoring and taking necessary actions about the ships.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 275: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Whenever a new ship arrives, or when a ship leaves or gets destroyed, another office could be one that manages the containers on the ships.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 276: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: They take care of containers that are damaged or fall off ships.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 277: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So these offices are, number one, continuously on the lookout for the status of the ships, and two, takes necessary actions to remediate the situation.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 278: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In the Kubernetes terms, a controller is a process that continuously monitors the state of various components within the system, and works towards bringing the whole system to the desired functioning state.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 279: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, the node controller is responsible for monitoring the status of the notes and taking necessary actions to keep the applications running.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 280: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It does that through the cube API server.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 281: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The node controller checks the status of the nodes every five seconds.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 282: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That way the node controller can monitor the health of the nodes.

### Evidence 283: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If it stops receiving heartbeat from a node, the node is marked as unreachable, but it waits for 40s before marking it unreachable.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 284: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: After a node is marked unreachable, it gives it five minutes to come back up.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 285: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If it doesn't, it removes the pods assigned to that node and provisions them on the healthy ones.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 286: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E15

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If the pods are part of a replica set, the next controller is the replication controller.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Architecture

### Evidence 287: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/014_Kube Controller Manager.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 014_Kube Controller Manager.txt

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 033_Services.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When we create a service without us having to do any additional configuration, Kubernetes automatically creates a service that spans across all the nodes in the cluster and maps the target port to the same node port on all the nodes in the cluster.

### Evidence 412: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/033_Services.extraction.md::E114

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: To summarize, in any case, whether it be a single pod on a single node, multiple pods on a single node, or multiple pods on multiple nodes, the service is created exactly the same without you having to do any additional steps during the service creation.

### Evidence 470: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So far in this course, we've created objects such as pods, deployments, and services in our cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 471: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the cluster is first set up, Kubernetes creates a set of pods and services for its internal purpose, such as those required by the networking solution, the DNS service, etc. to isolate these from the user and to prevent you from accidentally deleting or modifying these services, Kubernetes creates them under another namespace created at cluster startup Dub named kube system.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Networking, Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 472: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If your environment is small or you're learning and playing around with a small cluster, you shouldn't really have to worry about namespaces.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 473: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, as of when you grow and use a Kubernetes cluster for enterprise or production purposes, you may want to consider the use of namespaces.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 474: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, if you wanted to use the same cluster for both dev and production environment, but at the same time isolate the resources between them, you can create a different namespace for each of them.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 475: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 038_Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, for the web pod in the default namespace to connect to the database in the dev environment or namespace, use the service name dot namespace svc cluster dot local format that would be DB service, dot dev, dot svc cluster local.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Configuration, Architecture, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 476: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/038_Namespaces.extraction.md::E38

### Evidence 480: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The declarative approach would be to create a set of files that defines the expected state of the applications and services on a Kubernetes cluster, and with a single kubectl apply command, Kubernetes should be able to read the configuration files and decide by itself what needs to be done to bring the infrastructure to the expected state.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 481: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/02_Core Concepts/041_Imperative vs Declarative.extraction.md::E93
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 02_Core Concepts / 041_Imperative vs Declarative.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So for more details on the different approaches to managing a Kubernetes cluster, get yourself familiarized with the Kubernetes documentation pages, and in the upcoming lab exercises, try to use imperative approach in solving questions.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So when the scheduler tries to place this pod on node one, it goes through node one can now only accept pods that can tolerate the taint blue.

### Evidence 551: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/057_Taints and Tolerations.extraction.md::E43
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 057_Taints and Tolerations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There are three taint effects no schedule, which means the pods will not be scheduled on the node, which is what we have been discussing.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 552: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/057_Taints and Tolerations.extraction.md::E44
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 057_Taints and Tolerations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Prefer no schedule, which means the system will try to avoid placing a pod on the node, but that is not guaranteed.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 553: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/057_Taints and Tolerations.extraction.md::E45
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 057_Taints and Tolerations.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And third is no execute, which means that new pods will not be scheduled on the node and existing pods on the node, if any will be evicted if they do not tolerate the taint.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 554: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/057_Taints and Tolerations.extraction.md::E46

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: An example command would be to taint node node one with the key value pair app equals blue and an effect of no schedule tolerations are added to pods to add a toleration to a pod.

### Evidence 757: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/063_Lab Solution - Node Affinity (Optional).extraction.md::E66
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 03_Scheduling / 063_Lab Solution - Node Affinity (Optional).txt

### Evidence 814: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/03_Scheduling/064_Taints and Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E21

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Content: If we deploy this server externally on our own, and that is not a part of a deployment in Kubernetes cluster, then we can simply provide a URL path to that server like this.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Security-RBAC, Architecture

- Multi-concept tags: Services, Security-RBAC, Architecture

- Type: Concept

### Evidence 1004: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Or more importantly, what would you like to monitor?
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1005: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: I'd like to know node level metrics, such as the number of nodes in the cluster, how many of them are healthy, as well as performance metrics such as CPU, memory, network, and disk utilization, as well as pod level metrics such as the number of pods and the performance metrics of each pod, such as the CPU and memory consumption on them.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Networking, Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1006: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So we need a solution that will monitor these metrics, store them, and provide analytics around this data.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1007: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As of this recording, Kubernetes does not come with a full featured built in monitoring solution.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1008: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, there are a number of open source solutions available today, such as Metrics Server, Prometheus, Elastic Stack, and proprietary solutions like Datadog and Dynatrace.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1009: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Hipster was one of the original projects that enabled monitoring and analysis features for Kubernetes.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1010: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E9

- Type: Warning/Pitfall

### Evidence 1012: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You can have one metric server per Kubernetes cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1013: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The metrics server retrieves metrics from each of the Kubernetes nodes and pods, aggregates them, and stores them in memory.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1014: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Note that the metrics server is only an in-memory monitoring solution and does not store the metrics on the disk, and as a result, you cannot see historical performance data.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 1015: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt

- Content: For that, you must rely on one of the advanced monitoring solutions we talked about earlier in this lecture.

- Type: Concept

### Evidence 1017: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Kubernetes runs an agent on each node known as the Kubelet, which is responsible for receiving instructions from the Kubernetes API, master server, and running pods on the nodes.

### Evidence 1018: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E17

- Content: The Kubelet also contains a subcomponent known as the C advisor or Container Advisor.

### Evidence 1019: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/04_Logging & Monitoring/089_Monitor Cluster Components.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 04_Logging & Monitoring / 089_Monitor Cluster Components.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: C advisor is responsible for retrieving performance metrics from Pods and exposing them through the Kubelet API to make the metrics available for the metrics server.

### Evidence 1138: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, depending upon how you deploy those pods, your users may be impacted.

### Evidence 1139: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, since you have multiple replicas of the blue pod, the users accessing the blue application are not impacted as they're being served through the other blue pod that's online.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 1140: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, users accessing the green pod are impacted as that was the only pod running the green application.

### Evidence 1141: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, what does Kubernetes do in this case?
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1142: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If the node came back online immediately, then the cube control process starts and the pods come back online.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1143: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, if the node was down for more than five minutes, then the pods are terminated from that node.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1144: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Well, Kubernetes considers them as dead.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1145: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E13
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If the pods were part of a replica set, then they're recreated on other nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1146: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E14
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The time it waits for a pod to come back online is known as the pod eviction timeout and is set on the controller manager with a default value of five minutes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1147: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E15
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: In modern Kubernetes, pod eviction is handled through node taints and pod tolerations.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 1148: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When a node becomes not ready or unreachable, Kubernetes applies no execute taints and pods are evicted after a grace period unless they tolerate those taints.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 1149: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E17
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So whenever a node goes offline, the master node waits for up to five minutes before considering the node dead.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1150: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E18
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the node comes back online after the pod eviction timeout, it comes up blank without any pod scheduled on it.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 1151: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E19
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Since the blue pod was part of a replica set, it had a new pod created on another node.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1152: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, since the green pod was not part of a replica set, it's just gone.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1153: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E21
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Thus, if you have maintenance tasks to be performed on a node, if you know that the workloads running on the node have other replicas, and if it's okay that they go down for a short period of time, and if you're sure the node will come back online within five minutes, you can make a quick upgrade and reboot.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1154: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1155: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Well, you cannot for sure say it is going to be back at all.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1156: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/131_OS Upgrades.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 131_OS Upgrades.txt

- Content: When you drain a node, the pods are gracefully terminated from the node that they're on and recreated on another.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The components can be at different release versions, since the kube API server is the primary component in the control plane, and that is the component that all other components talk to.

### Evidence 1206: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: None of the other components should ever be at a version higher than the kube API server.

### Evidence 1207: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The controller, manager and scheduler can be at one version lower.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 1208: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So if kube API server was at EKS, controller managers and kube schedulers can be at x minus one and the kubelet and kube proxy components can be at two versions lower x minus two.

### Evidence 1209: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Architecture

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt

### Evidence 1235: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1236: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E37

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 1237: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E38

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt

### Evidence 1238: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E39

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It is now time to upgrade the worker nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1247: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E48
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There are different strategies available to upgrade the worker nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1248: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E49
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One is to upgrade all of them at once, but then your pods are down and users are no longer able to access the applications.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture

### Evidence 1249: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E50
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Once the upgrade is complete, the nodes are back up, new pods are scheduled, and users can resume access.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 1250: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: That's one strategy that requires downtime.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1251: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E52
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The second strategy is to upgrade one node at a time.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1252: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E53
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So going back to the state where we have our master upgraded and nodes waiting to be upgraded, we first upgrade the first node where the workloads move to the second and third node, and users are served from there.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1253: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E54
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Once the first node is upgraded and back up, we then update the second node where the workloads move to the first and third nodes, and finally the third node where the workloads are shared between the first two.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1254: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E55
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Until we have all nodes upgraded to a newer version, we then follow the same procedure to upgrade the nodes from 1.11 to 1.12 and then 1.13.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1255: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E56
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: A third strategy would be to add new nodes to the cluster nodes with newer software version.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 1256: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.extraction.md::E57
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 135_Cluster Upgrade Introduction.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is especially convenient if you're on a cloud environment, where you can easily provision new nodes and decommission old ones.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Content: So far in this course, we have deployed a number of different applications on our Kubernetes cluster using deployment pods and service definition files.

- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 138_Backup and Restore Methods.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We know that the etcd cluster is where all cluster related information is stored.

### Evidence 1471: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/138_Backup and Restore Methods.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 138_Backup and Restore Methods.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And if your applications are configured with persistent storage, then that is another candidate for backups.
- Multi-concept tags: Storage, Architecture

### Evidence 1472: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/138_Backup and Restore Methods.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 138_Backup and Restore Methods.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: With respect to resources that we created in the cluster, at times we use the imperative way of creating an object by executing a command, such as while creating a namespace or a secret or configmap, or at times for exposing applications.
- Multi-concept tags: Security-RBAC, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1473: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/138_Backup and Restore Methods.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 138_Backup and Restore Methods.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And at times we used the declarative approach by first creating a definition file and then running the kube control apply command on that file.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 1474: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/06_Cluster Maintenance/138_Backup and Restore Methods.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 06_Cluster Maintenance / 138_Backup and Restore Methods.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is the preferred approach if you want to save your configuration, because now you have all the objects required for a single application in the form of object definition files in a single folder.

### Evidence 1567: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/07_Security/148_TLS in Kubernetes.extraction.md::E22
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 07_Security / 148_TLS in Kubernetes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So the two primary requirements are to have all the various services within the cluster to use server certificates and all clients to use client certificates to verify they are who they say they are.

- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 09_Networking / 231_Ingress.txt

### Evidence 2318: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/09_Networking/231_Ingress.extraction.md::E89

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Ingress controllers have additional intelligence built into them to monitor the Kubernetes cluster for new definitions or ingress resources, and configure the nginx server accordingly, and nginx controller is deployed as just another deployment in Kubernetes.

### Evidence 2320: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/09_Networking/231_Ingress.extraction.md::E107

- Content: As mentioned before, the ingress controllers have additional intelligence built into them to monitor the Kubernetes cluster for ingress resources and configure the underlying nginx server when something is changed.

- Content: When a controller manager process is configured, you may specify the leader elect option, which is by default set to true.

### Evidence 2502: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/10_Design and Install a Kubernetes Cluster/243_Configure High Availability.extraction.md::E50
- Type: Architecture

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: With this option, when the controller manager process starts, it tries to gain a lease or a lock on an endpoint object in Kubernetes named as Kube Controller Manager endpoint.

### Evidence 2503: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/10_Design and Install a Kubernetes Cluster/243_Configure High Availability.extraction.md::E51
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 10_Design and Install a Kubernetes Cluster / 243_Configure High Availability.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Whichever process first updates the endpoint with its information, gains the lease, and becomes the active of the two.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 2504: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/10_Design and Install a Kubernetes Cluster/243_Configure High Availability.extraction.md::E52
- Type: Concept

- Content: It holds the lock for the lease duration specified using the lease duration option, which is by default set to 15 seconds.

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 10_Design and Install a Kubernetes Cluster / 244_ETCD in HA.txt

- Content: In this case, the group with four nodes have quorum and continues to operate normally.

- Content: However, if the network got partitioned in a different way, resulting in nodes being distributed equally between the two, each group now has three nodes only, but since we originally had six nodes, the quorum for the cluster to stay alive is four.
- Multi-concept tags: Networking, Architecture

- Content: But if you look at the groups here, neither of these groups have four managers to meet the quorum, so it results in a failed cluster.

### Evidence 2938: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/14_Troubleshooting/289_Control Plane Failure.extraction.md::E2
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 14_Troubleshooting / 289_Control Plane Failure.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We start by checking the status of the nodes in the cluster, see if they are all healthy, then check the status of the pods running on the cluster.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture, Troubleshooting

### Evidence 2939: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/14_Troubleshooting/289_Control Plane Failure.extraction.md::E3
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 14_Troubleshooting / 289_Control Plane Failure.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If we had control plane components deployed as pods in case of a cluster deployed with the kube ADM tool, then we can check to make sure that the pods in the kube system namespace are running, or else if the control plane components are deployed as services, as in our case, then check the status of the services such as the kube API server, the controller, manager and scheduler on the master nodes, and the Kubelet and kube proxy service on the worker nodes.

### Evidence 3219: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/15_Other Topics/299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 15_Other Topics / 299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When you're working with production environments for Kubernetes, you will need to view information about hundreds of nodes and thousands of objects like deployments, pods and replica sets and services and secrets, etc. and you will be using kube control utility to view information about these objects.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Security-RBAC, Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 3220: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/15_Other Topics/299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 15_Other Topics / 299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Every time you run a kube control command, it interacts with Kubernetes API through the kube API server.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 3221: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/15_Other Topics/299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Architecture
- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 15_Other Topics / 299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The kube API server speaks the JSON language, so it returns the requested information in a JSON format.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, Cross-Cloud

### Evidence 3222: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests/15_Other Topics/299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.extraction.md::E30

- Source: certified-kubernetes-administrator-with-practice-tests / 15_Other Topics / 299_JSON Path in Kubernetes.txt

- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, CKA-CKAD

### Evidence 3470: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/027_Recap - Namespaces.extraction.md::E16
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So far in this course, we've created objects such as pods, deployments, and services in our cluster.

### Evidence 3471: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/027_Recap - Namespaces.extraction.md::E20
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 027_Recap - Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the cluster is first set up, Kubernetes creates a set of pods and services for its internal purpose, such as those required by the networking solution, the DNS service, etc. to isolate these from the user and to prevent you from accidentally deleting or modifying these services, Kubernetes creates them under another namespace created at cluster startup Order named kube system.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Networking, Architecture, Observability

### Evidence 3472: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/027_Recap - Namespaces.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Best Practice
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 027_Recap - Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: If your environment is small or you're learning and playing around with a small cluster, you shouldn't really have to worry about namespaces.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture

### Evidence 3473: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/027_Recap - Namespaces.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 027_Recap - Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, as of when you grow and use a Kubernetes cluster for enterprise or production purposes, you may want to consider the use of namespaces.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture

### Evidence 3474: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/02_Core Concepts/027_Recap - Namespaces.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 02_Core Concepts / 027_Recap - Namespaces.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: For example, if you wanted to use the same cluster for both dev and production environment, but at the same time isolate the resources between them, you can create a different namespace for each of them.

- Content: In this case, node one can only accept pod D, but it does not guarantee that pod D will always be placed on node one, since there are no taints or restrictions applied on the other two nodes, pod D may very well be placed on any of the other two nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3749: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/073_Node Affinity.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 073_Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We know that we have only set the label size to large and medium nodes, The smaller nodes don't even have the label set, so we don't really have to even check the value of the label, as long as we are sure we don't set a label size to the smaller nodes, using the exists operator will give us the same result.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3750: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/073_Node Affinity.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 073_Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The excess operator will simply check if the label size exists on the nodes, and you don't need the values section for that, as it does not compare the values.

- Content: We have three nodes and three pods, each in three colors blue, red, and green.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3891: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E3

- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The ultimate aim is to place the blue pod in the blue node, the red pod in the red node, and likewise for green.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3892: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Concept

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We are sharing the same Kubernetes cluster with other teams, so there are other pods in the cluster as well as other nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3893: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E5

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We do not want any other pod to be placed on our node.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3894: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Neither do we want our pods to be placed on their nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3895: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Let us first try to solve this problem using taints and tolerations.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3896: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: We apply a taint to the nodes, marking them with their colors blue, red and green, and we then set a toleration on the pods to tolerate the respective colors.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3897: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: When the pods are now created, the nodes ensure they only accept the pods with the right toleration, so the green pod ends up on the green node and the blue pod ends up on the blue node.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3898: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 03_Configuration / 076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: However, taints and tolerations does not guarantee that the pods will only prefer these nodes, so the red pods ends up on one of the other nodes that do not have a taint or toleration set.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Scheduling, Architecture

### Evidence 3899: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E11

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This is not desired.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: As such, a combination of taints and tolerations and node affinity rules can be used together to completely dedicate nodes for specific pods.

### Evidence 3909: certified-kubernetes-application-developer/03_Configuration/076_Taints & Tolerations vs Node Affinity.extraction.md::E21

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

- Type: Concept
- Source: certified-kubernetes-application-developer / 05_Observability / 093_Monitor and Debug Applications.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: I'd like to know node level metrics, such as the number of nodes in the cluster, how many of them are healthy, as well as performance metrics such as CPU, memory, network, and disk utilization, as well as pod level metrics such as the number of pods and the performance metrics of each pod, such as CPU and memory consumption on them.

- Type: Architecture
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 007_Kubernetes Architecture.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 4469: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/007_Kubernetes Architecture.extraction.md::E26
- Type: Exam Tip
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 007_Kubernetes Architecture.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Think of it this way when you have multiple nodes and multiple masters in your cluster, etcd stores all that information on all the nodes in the cluster in a distributed manner.

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Etcd is responsible for implementing locks within the cluster to ensure that there are no conflicts between the masters, the scheduler is responsible for distributing work or containers across multiple nodes.

### Evidence 4513: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E89
- Type: Architecture
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Remember that it works along with the Kubelet, so we know that the Kubelet is responsible for ensuring that the specific number of containers or pods are available on on node at a time.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 4514: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E90
- Type: Architecture
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 02_Kubernetes Overview / 008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So if you go through the CRI control utility and try and create containers with it, then eventually Kubelet is going to delete them because the Kubelet is unaware of some of those containers or pods that are created outside of its knowledge.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Architecture, Observability, On-Prem

### Evidence 4515: learn-kubernetes/02_Kubernetes Overview/008_Docker-vs-ContainerD.extraction.md::E104

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 4524: learn-kubernetes/03_Kubernetes Concepts/012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.extraction.md::E32

### Evidence 4525: learn-kubernetes/03_Kubernetes Concepts/012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.extraction.md::E36
- Type: Architecture
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 03_Kubernetes Concepts / 012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Earlier, we talked about the different components of Kubernetes that make up a control plane and worker nodes such as the API server, etcd key value store controllers and scheduler on the master or the control plane, and then you have the Kubernetes and container runtime on the worker nodes.
- Multi-concept tags: Scheduling, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 4526: learn-kubernetes/03_Kubernetes Concepts/012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.extraction.md::E37
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 03_Kubernetes Concepts / 012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, it would take a lot of time and effort to set up and install all of these various components on different systems, individually, by ourselves, which is why a mini cube bundles all of these different components into a single image, providing us a pre-configured single node Kubernetes cluster so we can get started in a matter of minutes.
- Multi-concept tags: Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 4527: learn-kubernetes/03_Kubernetes Concepts/012_Kubernetes Setup - Introduction and Minikube.extraction.md::E44

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And finally, to interact with the Kubernetes cluster, you must have the kubectl Kubernetes command line tool also installed on your machine.
- Multi-concept tags: Configuration, Architecture, On-Prem

### Evidence 4622: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E21

### Evidence 4623: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E22

- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt

### Evidence 4624: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E23
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 4625: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E24
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 4626: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E25
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 4628: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E27
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript

### Evidence 4629: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E28
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One of its use cases is to listen to a port on the node, and forward requests on that port to a port on the pod running the web application.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 4630: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E29
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: This type of service is known as a node port service, because the service listens to a port on the node and forward requests to the pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 4631: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E30
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: There are other kinds of services available which we will now discuss.

### Evidence 4632: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E31
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The first one is what we discussed already.

### Evidence 4633: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E32
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Node port where the service makes an internal pod accessible on a port on the node.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 4634: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E33
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The second is cluster IP and in this case the service creates a virtual IP inside the cluster to enable communication between different services, such as a set of front end servers to a set of back end backend servers.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

### Evidence 4635: learn-kubernetes/07_Services/039_Services - NodePort.extraction.md::E34
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 07_Services / 039_Services - NodePort.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: The third type is a load balancer, where it provisions a load balancer for our application in supported cloud providers.
- Multi-concept tags: Services, Architecture

- Type: Concept

### Evidence 5185: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E4
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Now, since we deployed pods directly, I want you guys to be aware that there are some limitations when you deploy pods directly.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5186: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E5
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: One there isn't a way to easily scale up the number of pods, so if we wanted instead of one instance of the voting app, we wanted five.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5187: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E6
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: It doesn't provide us a mechanism to easily scale up and scale down.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5188: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E7
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: On top of that, if one of our applications goes down, there's nothing watching over it to restart it or deploy a new instance.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5189: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E8
- Type: Warning/Pitfall
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: You don't get that functionality with just pods.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5190: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E9
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: And on top of that, there's no simple way to upgrade your application.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5191: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E10
- Type: Concept
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So if you want version two of the voting or result app, you would have to delete the old one.
- Multi-concept tags: Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5192: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E11
- Type: Implementation Step
- Source: learn-kubernetes / 08_Microservices Architecture / 048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.txt
- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: Deploy a brand new pod spec with the new version, and this could lead to user impact as your service would be down during that time.
- Multi-concept tags: Pods, Deployments, Services, Architecture

### Evidence 5193: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E12
- Type: Implementation Step

- Line Context: Lines 1-1 from transcript
- Content: So instead of deploying pods directly, what we want to do is we want to make use of other Kubernetes primitives like replica sets and deployments.

### Evidence 5194: learn-kubernetes/08_Microservices Architecture/048_Demo - Deploying Voting App on Kubernetes With Deployments.extraction.md::E13
